summary: DHH argues that open-plan offices are fundamentally hostile to productive work, benefiting only managers and photo ops while harming the vast majority of workers. He cites a Royal Society study showing that open offices actually decrease face-to-face collaboration — the opposite of their stated purpose — as people retreat into headphone cocoons and rely on messaging instead. Drawing from personal experience at early-2000s tech companies, he claims he never could have created Ruby on Rails in an open office environment. He presents a continuum from worst (mixed departments in one room) to least bad (small team rooms under six people), while arguing the entire concept remains an unquestioned default that squanders human health and potential.
key_points: The vast majority of workers dislike or hate open offices, yet they persist because managers — who don't need long uninterrupted focus time — are the ones designing office layouts
Research shows open offices actually decrease face-to-face collaboration, directly contradicting the primary justification for their existence
Open offices exist on a continuum of badness: mixed departments together is worst, same-function groups are less bad, and small teams under six people are least bad
The open office persists as an almost unquestioned default in tech despite an endless stream of studies showing it causes stress, conflict, and turnover
The real beneficiaries of open offices are investor tours, journalist interviews, and photo shoots — not the workers
money_quotes: Open offices are more appealing to people in management because they needn't protect their own time and attention as much.
Converting traditional offices with walls and doors and separation into open-plan offices causes face-to-face interaction to plummet, not rise.
People try to shield their attention (and sanity!) by retreating into headphone-clad cocoons, and instead rely on instant messaging or email to interact.
I feel quite comfortable stating that I would never have been able to create Ruby on Rails or any of my other software or creative achievements in such an environment.
It was a tyranny of interruption, distraction, and stress.
We're squandering human health and potential on an epic scale by forcing the vast majority of people who dislike or hate the open office into that configuration.

There's been an endless stream of studies showing that the open-plan office is a source of stress, conflict, and turnover. And yet it's still the default in tech. An almost unquestioned default. That's a fucking travesty.
key_insight: Open-plan offices persist not because they work but because the people who choose them — managers — don't suffer the consequences, while the workers who do suffer lack the power to change them.
